title: VP of Platform Engineering
message:
Subject: 5G scale + DataStax licensing — alternative worth exploring

Hi Karan,

I've been following T-Mobile's 5G expansion, and I'm guessing your subscriber data platform is feeling the strain. Tripling node count with per-node licensing is a brutal equation at telco scale.

Here's what caught my attention: we work with infrastructure teams running massive Cassandra deployments who faced the exact same problem. Most are surprised to learn they can replace 10 Cassandra nodes with 1 alternative—without touching a single line of application code.

The magic is a different architecture. No JVM, no GC pauses, shard-per-core design that hits 1M+ ops/sec per node. More importantly for your situation: consumption-based pricing, not per-node licensing. That model changes the economics dramatically when you're scaling subscriber data at your volume.

Since you're already on Cassandra with mature integrations, there's zero rewrite risk. Drop-in compatible, same CQL, same drivers.

title: Principal Database Architect
message:
Subject: Cassandra costs & compaction — what we're seeing in 2026 renewals

Hi Lin,

I reached out because your situation is one we're seeing repeatedly: DataStax renewals hitting hard, and compaction management eating up engineering cycles that should go elsewhere.

Given your background as a DataStax SE, you know exactly where the operational overhead lives. The JVM tuning, the compaction storms under write load, the per-node licensing stacking on top of infrastructure costs — that's the tax most teams don't realize they're paying until renewal time.

We've worked with companies like Comcast running Cassandra at scale, and the pattern is consistent: a drop-in Cassandra replacement can cut node count by 80–90% while cutting compaction overhead in half. Same CQL, same drivers, no rewrite. But what matters for your 2026 decision is that the economics flip entirely — consumption-based licensing instead of per-node tax, and significantly lower storage amplification.

I'm not going to oversell this. With your DataStax background, you'll spot the gaps fast. But if exploring a real alternative before renewal makes sense, I'd rather have a 15-minute conversation now than watch this drag into Q1.

Are you open to a brief technical call sometime in the next week?

Lin

title: Senior Backend Engineer
message:
Subject: Eliminating Q4 GC pauses for your listings platform

Hi Jake,

I've been following eBay's scale challenges with Cassandra—managing 2B+ listings across peak season is no small feat, especially when JVM GC pauses start tanking your listing API latency in Q4.

We work with backend teams handling similar catalog-scale problems. The core issue is the JVM itself: garbage collection becomes a bottleneck the moment your traffic spikes. Most teams end up throwing more nodes at the problem, which compounds licensing costs.

There's a simpler path. A few companies like Discord and Comcast replaced their Cassandra clusters with a C++ alternative that:

1. **Eliminates GC pauses entirely** — shard-per-core architecture means no stop-the-world collections during peak load
2. **Cuts infrastructure ~80–90%** — one node handles what takes 10 Cassandra nodes; plus, consumption-based licensing (no per-node tax like DataStax)

It's a drop-in replacement for Cassandra. Same CQL, same drivers, zero application rewrites. Zero-downtime migration is standard.

Given your Q4 timeline and cost pressures, might be worth a quick 20-min conversation to explore whether this fits your architecture.

title: Database Engineer
message:
Subject: Black Friday inventory latency — new approach

Hi Fatima,

I've been following Walmart's real-time inventory platform work — managing 500M+ SKUs at that scale, especially during peak season, is a genuine operational challenge.

I wanted to reach out because we've been working with teams facing the exact issue you mentioned: Cassandra compaction storms tanking read latency when it matters most. We've found that the root cause isn't usually the data model or query patterns — it's the JVM's garbage collection and how compaction strategies compete for IO during high write volume.

We built ScyllaDB as a drop-in Cassandra replacement (same CQL, same drivers, no rewrites) but with a shard-per-core C++ engine that eliminates GC pauses and reduces compaction overhead by ~50%. One team we worked with went from p99 latencies in the 200ms range down to single-digit milliseconds under their peak load — and cut their node count by 80%.

On the licensing side, since you mentioned DataStax's per-node model, ScyllaDB Enterprise shifts to consumption-based pricing with no per-node tax. That alone changes your peak-capacity economics.

I'd like to show you a 15-minute technical overview of how this works in practice — specifically for inventory platforms. Would next week work?

Best,
[Your name]
